text: The surgeon visualizes the lesion on the sigmoid colon and begins the procedure using a middle to lateral approach.
During the dissection, the surgeon identifies the left ureter, ensuring its preservation while continuing the procedure.
The surgeon dissects the anterior mesenteric artery approximately 1.5 centimeters from the aorta, using precise technique to separate the artery from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a 45mm wrist instrument with a vascular recharge to section the targeted area, likely involving the left iliac vessel as previously set.
The surgeon continues the dissection using a monopolar scissor and bipolar fenestrated graspers, manipulating the tissue around the targeted left iliac vessel.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric vein, applies clips to secure it, and then sections the vein, completing the vascular division.
The surgeon employs an extended medial-to-lateral approach to mobilize the splenic flexure, carefully manipulating the surrounding tissue to achieve adequate exposure.

Following the ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein at the lower border of the pancreas, the surgeon separates the superior surface of the pancreas from the retroperitoneum, opening the lesser sac.
The surgeon divides the gastrocolic and pancreatic mesocolic attachments along the tail of the pancreas, separating these structures from surrounding tissues.
A sterile gauze is placed at the splenic hilum before the sectioning of the gastrocolic ligament.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the entire left colon by dissecting the total fascia, separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon transects the rectum at a point at least five centimeters distal to the colic cancer, ensuring a clear margin from the tumor.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to prepare the rectal stump for transaction, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon transects the rectum using a single 45mm endobriste grain cartridge, completing the division of the rectal tissue.
The surgeon extracts the colon through a five-centimeter incision, using a funny-seal technique.
The surgeon uses Indocyanine Green fluorescence angiography to identify the proximal transaction margin after dividing the mesentery, before proceeding with sectioning.
The surgeon inserts the EEA stapler at the rectal stump, ensuring good vascular supply, and secures it with pearl sutures.

The surgeon performs a colon-heptal end-to-end anastomosis, using indocyanine green fluorescence angiography to recheck the vascular viability of the intestinal tissue, confirming a good vascular supply.
